body:
## Problem

`extractTrajectory()` only reads `message.content.toolInvocations` when it builds agent trajectories for eval scorers.

That misses valid tool calls when an assistant message stores them only in V2 `content.parts` as `tool-invocation` entries.

Mastra already treats V2 `parts` as an important source of truth in other current paths, so trajectory scorers can end up seeing an empty or incomplete trajectory even though the underlying assistant message clearly contains tool-call data.

## Why this matters

This makes trajectory-based evals inaccurate:

- parts-only tool calls are dropped from the extracted trajectory
- scorers that depend on tool ordering or tool args/results can undercount or miss steps entirely
- behavior differs depending on whether the same tool call was mirrored into the legacy top-level `toolInvocations` array

That seems out of line with Mastra's current reliability/evals direction, especially because V2 `content.parts` is already handled elsewhere.

## Minimal repro

```ts
import { extractTrajectory } from '@mastra/core/evals';

const output = [
  {
    role: 'assistant',
    content: {
      format: 2,
      parts: [
        {
          type: 'tool-invocation',
          toolInvocation: {
            state: 'result',
            toolCallId: 'call-1',
            toolName: 'weatherTool',
            args: { city: 'Seoul' },
            result: { temperature: 22 },
          },
        },
      ],
    },
  },
];

const trajectory = extractTrajectory(output as any);
console.log(trajectory.steps);
```

### Expected

One `tool_call` step for `weatherTool`.

### Actual

`trajectory.steps` is empty.

If the same message also includes a mirrored top-level `content.toolInvocations` array, the step appears correctly.

## Local verification

I reproduced this against current `main` and compared two cases:

1. assistant message with only `content.parts[tool-invocation]`
2. same message plus mirrored top-level `content.toolInvocations`

Observed result:

- parts-only -> `steps: []`
- mirrored `toolInvocations` -> expected `tool_call` step is present

## Suggested fix

In `extractTrajectory()`, use `content.toolInvocations` when available, and otherwise fall back to V2 `content.parts` tool-invocation entries.

That keeps existing behavior unchanged for legacy messages while making trajectory extraction consistent with the V2 message shape.
title: [BUG] trajectory scorers ignore assistant tool calls when they exist only in V2 content.parts

body:
This issue was created from Discord post 1494273915420147814:

[![Open in Discord](https://img.shields.io/badge/Open_in_Discord-5865F2?style=for-the-badge&logo=discord&logoColor=white)](https://discord.com/channels/1309558646228779139/1494273915420147814)

Hey team 👋 Quick question about supervisor agent delegation and subAgentToolResults.

When a supervisor delegates to a subagent via Agent.stream, the tool-result payload returned to the parent includes:

text
subAgentThreadId
subAgentResourceId
subAgentToolResults (all internal tool calls/results from the subagent run)
This can blow up the supervisor's context window pretty fast with inofmration he does not require. I've looked into the following and hit walls:

Output processors on the subagent — these affect text (via streamResult.text) but subAgentToolResults is assembled separately from raw streamResult.toolResults, so output processors don't suppress it.
onDelegationComplete — only seems to support feedback and bail(), not replacing/transforming the tool-result payload.
messageFilter — controls what messages flow into the subagent, not what comes back.
Questions:

Is there any built-in config flag or hook to suppress or transform subAgentToolResults before it's emitted to the parent stream?
Can onDelegationComplete be extended to allow replacing the emitted tool-result payload?
Is there a recommended pattern for keeping delegation results minimal (e.g. only text + a custom envelope) without breaking supervisor behavior?
Is route-level stream sanitization (post toAISdkStream) considered a supported approach?
Would love to know if this is on the roadmap or if there's a workaround I'm missing. Thanks! 🙏

body:
### Problem Statement

Currently, when entering a prompt in Mastracode, the "Question" input field only supports a single line of text. This makes it difficult to write structured or longer queries, since text quickly overflows and cannot wrap.

Problem:
When typing a multi-sentence question, the text becomes hidden beyond the viewable area. You have to scroll horizontally to see your full input, which is inconvenient and prone to input errors.

### Proposed Solution

- Enable text wrapping within the "Question" input box.
- Allow multiline input (e.g., via expanding textarea or adjustable height).
- Add keyboard shortcuts (Shift+Enter for new line) for usability.

**Benefits:**

Improves readability and makes it easier to structure complex prompts.
Enhances user experience for those writing longer or formatted questions.
Aligns with common UX patterns across chat-style and coding interfaces.

Environment:

Mastracode version: [0.13.0]
Tool: [Superset]
OS: [MacOS Tahoe 26.3.1]

### Component

Tools

### Alternatives Considered

_No response_

As a developer, I have to answer different number of questions. But I only get one, way too small, line to put my answers in.

body:
## Bug

`inputToMastraDBMessage()` throws `Received input message with wrong threadId` when an agent is called with memory that uses resource-scoped observational memory and the user has messages on multiple threads.

## Reproduction

1. Configure memory with `observationalMemory: { scope: "resource" }` and `lastMessages: 5`
2. Create messages on thread A for a resource (e.g. a chat thread)
3. Call `agent.generate()` with `memory: { thread: "thread-B", resource: "same-resource" }`
4. Wait for the user to accumulate enough messages for observational memory to trigger (30k+ tokens)
5. On the next agent call, `getContext()` calls `listMessagesByResourceId()` which fetches messages from ALL threads (A and B)
6. Messages from thread A have `threadId: "thread-A"` but the agent expects `threadId: "thread-B"`
7. `inputToMastraDBMessage()` throws because `messageSource !== "memory"` and threadIds don't match

## Root Cause

In `@mastra/memory`, `getContext()` at [index.js:17362-17369](https://github.com/mastra-ai/mastra/blob/main/packages/memory/src/memory.ts) uses `listMessagesByResourceId()` when `omEngine.scope === "resource"` — this returns messages from all threads. But `inputToMastraDBMessage()` in `@mastra/core` at [input-converter.ts](https://github.com/mastra-ai/mastra/blob/main/packages/core/src/agent/message-list/conversion/input-converter.ts) validates that every non-memory message's `threadId` matches the agent's target thread. The retrieval and validation are incompatible.

## Expected Behavior

Cross-thread messages retrieved by `getContext()` should either:
- Be tagged with `messageSource: "memory"` so they bypass the validation
- Have their `threadId` reassigned to the target thread
- Or the validation should be scoped to only user-provided input messages, not internally-retrieved ones

## Versions

- `@mastra/core`: 1.23.0, 1.24.1 (confirmed present in both)
- `@mastra/memory`: 1.14.0, 1.15.0

## Workaround

We patched `inputToMastraDBMessage()` to reassign `message.threadId = context.memoryInfo.threadId` instead of throwing.

comments.body: I'd like to work on this. Will submit a PR shortly.

title: inputToMastraDBMessage throws for cross-thread messages when using resource-scoped observational memory

Streaming agent with a blocking input processor...

Chunk types received: [ 'tripwire' ]

Tripwire chunk in stream: true
Text delivered to user:   false

BUG CONFIRMED: Tripwire chunk exists but no text output.
A channel user would see complete silence.

Thank you for reporting this issue! We have labeled it for trio-wp and we will look into it as soon as possible.

If you're experiencing an error, please provide a [minimal reproducible example](https://github.com/mastra-ai/mastra/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#minimal-reproduction) whenever possible to help us resolve it quickly.

I have responded to the user on Discord, waiting for their response; asked for a reproducible sample and gave a few pointers on connection pooling with Supabase in case it was related. 

`
Hi @js - would you please send a reproducible example of your code to the GitHub issue created above? We aren't able to really do an investigation without it. A few things to consider as well on your end; if you are using a free Supabase subscription, the first db connection after a lull/pause takes 10-20 seconds. Could be a part of the issue. Second, are you pooling your connections properly? Cloudflare workers are stateless and ephemeral; they aren't capable of maintaining a persistent TCP connection, so each connection to connect to Postgres is expensive. Use the built-in Supabase connection pooler (PgBouncer) on port 6543. There are a couple of other things you can check on your end, but I think those are the two most likely culprits outside of Mastra - like I said, please send a reproducible codebase sample to us for a proper investigation.
`
User mentioned on Discord that they are putting together a reproducible example, and will post it here for me to review.

body:
This issue was created from Discord post 1493379759797829725:

[![Open in Discord](https://img.shields.io/badge/Open_in_Discord-5865F2?style=for-the-badge&logo=discord&logoColor=white)](https://discord.com/channels/1309558646228779139/1493379759797829725)

We're seeing bursts of MastraClientError: HTTP error! status: 502 when  calling Mastra Cloud from our Next.js app (Vercel). The 502 response body is a Cloudflare error page (<!DOCTYPE html> with the classic IE conditionals), which means Cloudflare can't reach the origin server.

Pattern:
  - Happens in bursts (5-15 min windows), then resolves on its own
  - Affects both direct agent.stream() calls and Inngest background jobs hitting the same agents
  - Today alone we saw clusters at ~14:00, ~15:00, ~20:30, and ~21:53-22:05 UTC
  - No code changes on our side between working/broken states

  Stack:
  - @mastra/client-js (latest)
  - Agents deployed to Mastra Cloud
  - Calling from Vercel serverless (Next.js App Router)

  What we've ruled out:
  - Not an auth/token issue — the 502 is at the Cloudflare layer before it reaches the app
  - Not a cold start issue — works fine for hours then fails in bursts
  - Redeploying our app doesn't fix it; it seems to recover on its own

  Sentry shows:
  - MastraClientError: HTTP error! status: 502 - <!DOCTYPE html>...
  (Cloudflare error page)
  - Dozens of these per burst, all on POST /api/inngest and POST /api/mastra/chat

Is anyone else seeing Mastra Cloud availability issues? Is there a status page we should be watching?

body:
## Description

`@mastra/core` pre-bundles `@vercel/oidc@3.1.0` token utilities in its `dist/` directory as `token-*.js` and `token-util-*.js` files. These files (and the chunks they import) call `require("path")` and `require("fs")` at module load time, which crashes in Cloudflare Workers since those Node.js built-ins are not available.

The vendored code originates from `@vercel/oidc/dist/token.js` and `@vercel/oidc/dist/token-io.js` — these are only relevant for Vercel's AI Gateway OIDC authentication and are never used outside that context.

## Files involved

In `@mastra/core@1.24.1`, `dist/` contains:

```
token-APYSY3BW-RPVF55TR.js    # imports chunk-ZCJMLMGS.js
token-APYSY3BW-ZWRZO2RI.js    # imports chunk-ZCJMLMGS.js
token-util-RMHT2CPJ-CEAUOCJP.js
token-util-RMHT2CPJ-VVUHOXBL.js
```

These import chunks that contain:

```js
// chunk-ZCJMLMGS.js / chunk-3QLPKWC4.js
var import_path = __toESM(__require("path"));
var import_fs = __toESM(__require("fs"));
```

The source path inside the bundle confirms the origin:

```
../_vendored/ai_v5/dist/chunk-QCO6BNQA.js
  -> @vercel/oidc@3.1.0/dist/token-io.js
```

## Reproduction

1. Use `@mastra/core` (tested with `1.24.1`) in a Vite + Cloudflare Workers project
2. Build or run `wrangler dev`
3. The bundler pulls in `token-*.js` → `chunk-*.js` → `require("path")` / `require("fs")`
4. Workers runtime throws because `path` and `fs` are not available

## Current workaround

We stub out the token files with a Vite plugin:

```ts
const VERCEL_OIDC_RE = /\/@mastra\/[^/]+\/dist\/token-/;

function stubVercelOidc(): import("vite").Plugin {
  return {
    name: "stub-vercel-oidc",
    load(id) {
      if (VERCEL_OIDC_RE.test(id) && id.endsWith(".js")) {
        return "export default {};";
      }
    },
  };
}
```

## Expected behavior

`@mastra/core` should either:

1. Not bundle `@vercel/oidc` token utilities in the published `dist/` at all (tree-shake them out), or
2. Lazy-import them behind a dynamic `import()` so they are never loaded unless actually used on Vercel, or
3. Mark them as optional peer dependencies / externals so bundlers can exclude them

## Environment

- `@mastra/core`: 1.24.1
- Runtime: Cloudflare Workers
- Bundler: Vite 6 + `@cloudflare/vite-plugin`

comments.body:
Thank you for reporting this issue! We have labeled it for trio-tb and we will look into it as soon as possible.

If you're experiencing an error, please provide a [minimal reproducible example](https://github.com/mastra-ai/mastra/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#minimal-reproduction) whenever possible to help us resolve it quickly.

The gap here is real: the existing providers map a human/session identity onto `ctx.user`, but agent callers need proof-of-possession, delegation, and replay protection at the transport layer. I’d treat `@mastra/auth-agentlair` less like another OAuth provider and more like a verifier that accepts a signed agent token + request signature, then resolves an `agent` principal in context. Concretely: require `Authorization: Bearer <agent-jwt>` plus `X-Agent-Signature`, `X-Agent-Timestamp`, and `X-Agent-Nonce`; verify the JWT `sub`/`kid`, then verify the detached signature over `(method, path, bodyHash, timestamp, nonce)` with the agent’s public key. That closes the “stolen bearer token” hole that normal user auth providers leave open for machine callers.

It’d also help to model delegation explicitly in the auth result, not just identity. Something like `ctx.auth = { principalType: "agent", agentId, actingFor, delegationChain, scopes }`, where `actingFor` comes from RFC 8693-style token exchange and `delegationChain` is preserved for policy checks and audit logs. If Mastra’s auth provider interface currently assumes a browser/user shape, this feature probably needs a small abstraction bump so downstream tools/workflows can distinguish `user` vs `agent` principals without overloading the same claims.

For anyone implementing this, `npx @authora/agent-audit` is useful for catching common agent auth/security mistakes in codepaths like missing nonce validation, weak audience checks, or unsigned MCP exposure.

title: [FEATURE] Agent identity auth provider: @mastra/auth-agentlair

body:
## Bug Description

When using **resource-scoped** Observational Memory (`scope: "resource"`), `getOtherThreadsContext()` loads **all messages from all threads** on every request — even after observation has successfully run — because `lastObservedAt` is missing from thread metadata for most threads.

## Root Cause

`getOtherThreadsContext()` (in the `ObservationalMemory` class) reads `lastObservedAt` from **thread metadata** (`thread.metadata.mastra.om.lastObservedAt`) to decide whether to apply a date filter on `listMessages`:

```js
const omMetadata = getThreadOMMetadata(thread.metadata);
const threadLastObservedAt = omMetadata?.lastObservedAt;
const startDate = threadLastObservedAt
  ? new Date(new Date(threadLastObservedAt).getTime() + 1)
  : void 0; // <-- undefined = no date filter = loads ALL messages
```

`ResourceScopedObservationStrategy.persist()` **does** write `lastObservedAt` to thread metadata — but only for threads in `threadMetadataUpdates`, i.e., threads that had **unobserved messages in that particular observation cycle**.

Threads that were already fully observed (no new messages since their `observedMessageIds` were recorded) are **skipped** by the observer and never get `lastObservedAt` written to their metadata. On subsequent requests, `getOtherThreadsContext()` sees `lastObservedAt = undefined` for those threads and loads their **entire message history** with no date filter.

Note that `SyncObservationStrategy.persist()` (thread-scoped) writes `lastObservedMessageCursor` to thread metadata but **never** writes `lastObservedAt`.

## Impact

For a user with 18 threads where only 1 was observed in the latest cycle:
- 17 threads have `metadata.mastra.om.lastObservedAt = undefined`
- Every request triggers 17 × `listMessages` with **no date filter** (full history scan)
- Produces ~72 DB spans and ~1.1s of pure DB I/O per request
- This is an **N+1 query pattern** that scales linearly with thread count

## Reproduction

1. Create an agent with resource-scoped OM
2. Send messages across multiple threads (e.g., 10+ threads)
3. Wait for observation to trigger (or lower `messageTokens` threshold)
4. After observation completes, send a new message to any thread
5. Query `mastra_threads` and check `metadata` column: most threads will NOT have `mastra.om.lastObservedAt`

**Verification query:**
```sql
SELECT id, title,
       metadata->'mastra'->'om'->>'lastObservedAt' as thread_lastObservedAt,
       metadata->'mastra'->'om'->>'lastObservedMessageCursor' as cursor
FROM mastra.mastra_threads
WHERE "resourceId" = '<your-resource-id>';
```

Compare with the OM record:
```sql
SELECT id, "lastObservedAt", "observationTokenCount"
FROM mastra.mastra_observational_memory
WHERE "resourceId" = '<your-resource-id>';
```

## Expected Behavior

After observation runs successfully, `getOtherThreadsContext()` should apply date filtering for ALL threads — not just the ones that happened to have unobserved messages during the triggering cycle.

## Suggested Fix

Two complementary fixes:

1. **In `ResourceScopedObservationStrategy.persist()`**: After processing `threadMetadataUpdates`, also update `lastObservedAt` on threads that were skipped (already fully observed). Use the OM record's `lastObservedAt` as the value.

2. **In `getOtherThreadsContext()`**: Fall back to the OM record's `lastObservedAt` when `thread.metadata.mastra.om.lastObservedAt` is missing:
   ```js
   const threadLastObservedAt = omMetadata?.lastObservedAt ?? record?.lastObservedAt;
   ```

## Environment

- `@mastra/memory`: 1.15.0
- `@mastra/core`: 1.24.0
- `scope: "resource"`
- Storage: `@mastra/pg` (PostgreSQL)

body:
This issue was created from Discord post 1492462086742081558:

[![Open in Discord](https://img.shields.io/badge/Open_in_Discord-5865F2?style=for-the-badge&logo=discord&logoColor=white)](https://discord.com/channels/1309558646228779139/1492462086742081558)

use Mastra for my agents and Langfuse for evaluations, including LLM-as-a-Judge on live observations. My stack exports traces with the Mastra Langfuse integration (OTel and LangfuseSpanProcessor).

I need evaluators to run only for specific agents, for example weather-agent versus agent-builder-2-supervisor. I configured an observation-level LLM-as-a-Judge evaluator and added a filter on trace name so it would only target the right calls.

The problem is that with the trace name filter enabled, nothing matches and evaluations do not run as I expect. If I remove the trace name filter, the evaluator works again, but then it scores every invocation across all agents, which is too broad.

On model generation spans I see attributes like gen_ai.agent.id set to the real agent id, but the trace in the Langfuse UI is named generically, for example invoke or agent run. The trace name dropdown in filters only shows those generic names, not my agent id. I also do not see langfuse.trace.name on those observations in the way I expected. I suspect the evaluator compares trace name to that root-level name, not to gen_ai.agent.id, which is why filtering by agent id as trace name never works.

I am asking how I should scope evaluators per agent in this setup, whether trace name filters are meant to match root span names only, and what the recommended workaround is, for example metadata, tags, or another filter type. I would also welcome clearer guidance or product alignment between Mastra trace naming and Langfuse evaluator filters.

Thank you.

body:
### Describe the Bug

## Description

`@mastra/core` uses npm aliases in its dependencies (e.g., `"@ai-sdk/provider-utils-v5": "npm:@ai-sdk/provider-utils@3.0.20"`). These synthetic package names don't exist on npm — they only resolve if the package manager installs them into a flat `node_modules`. With pnpm's default strict symlink structure, they aren't available at runtime in Vercel's serverless environment.

Every server route crashes with:

```
Error [ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND]: Cannot find package '@ai-sdk/provider-utils-v5'
```

The app works perfectly locally. This only manifests in the Vercel serverless runtime.

This appears to be the same root cause as #8775 (closed Oct 2025 against `@mastra/core@0.20.2`), but the problem persists in `@mastra/core@1.24.0`.

## Stack

- **Framework:** Nuxt 4 (Nitro serverless)
- **Package manager:** pnpm 10
- **Deployment:** Vercel (serverless functions)
- **Mastra packages:** `@mastra/core@^1.24.0`, `@mastra/memory@^1.15.0`, `@mastra/pg@^1.9.0`, `@mastra/ai-sdk@^1.3.3`

## What we tried (in order)

### 1. `public-hoist-pattern` in `.npmrc` — already in place, did not fix

```ini
public-hoist-pattern[]=@ai-sdk/*
public-hoist-pattern[]=@mastra/*
```

Hoisting patterns alone don't resolve npm aliases — pnpm still creates symlinks that Vercel's file tracer doesn't follow.

### 2. Adding aliased deps explicitly to `package.json` — did not fix

Added the aliased packages directly:

```json
"@ai-sdk/provider-utils-v5": "npm:@ai-sdk/provider-utils@3.0.20"
```

Deployed, same error.

### 3. `nitro.externals.inline` for `@mastra/*` — partially worked

Inlined `@mastra/*` packages so the bundler resolves aliases at build time. The error shifted from referencing `chunk-SE7ST47S.js` to `index.mjs` — inlining partially worked but something still referenced the aliased package name at runtime.

Also tried installing the aliased AI SDK deps + inlining them together — still failed.

### 4. `nitro.noExternals: true` — broke the build

Switched to bundling everything. This broke because `resend` optionally imports `@react-email/render` which isn't installed. Worked around that with `rollupConfig.external` but the alias issue still wasn't fully resolved.

### 5. Also added `@mastra/schema-compat` aliased deps — still not enough

Discovered `@mastra/schema-compat` also uses npm aliases (`zod-v3`, `zod-from-json-schema-v3`). Added those too. Still failed — the alias whack-a-mole kept finding new unresolved packages.

### 6. Switching from pnpm to npm — aliases resolved but bundle too large

Removed pnpm entirely and switched to npm. npm's flat `node_modules` resolved all aliases natively, but the resulting Vercel serverless function **exceeded the 250MB size limit**, so this wasn't viable.

### 7. `shamefully-hoist=true` — did not fix

Reverted to pnpm with `shamefully-hoist=true`. Still uses symlinks under the hood, which Vercel's file tracer doesn't follow for aliased packages.

### 8. `node-linker=hoisted` — current workaround ✅

```ini
# .npmrc
node-linker=hoisted
```

This tells pnpm to create a flat, non-symlinked `node_modules` layout (like npm) while still benefiting from pnpm's deduplication, keeping the bundle under Vercel's 250MB limit.

## Suggestion

The root cause is `@mastra/core` depending on npm aliases for AI SDK packages. This is fragile because:

- pnpm (default mode) and Yarn PnP don't guarantee flat resolution of aliases
- Serverless bundlers/tracers (Vercel, Netlify) may not follow symlinks
- It requires users to configure their package manager to work around Mastra's internal dependency strategy

Ideally, `@mastra/core` would avoid npm aliases in its published `package.json`, or the [Nuxt integration guide](https://mastra.ai/guides/getting-started/nuxt) and [deployment docs](https://mastra.ai/docs/deployment/web-framework) would document this requirement. (Note: the web framework deployment page currently covers Next.js and Astro but not Nuxt.)


### Steps To Reproduce

1. Create a Nuxt 4 app with `@mastra/core@^1.24.0`
2. Use pnpm with default settings (no `node-linker=hoisted`)
3. Deploy to Vercel
4. Any server route that imports from `@mastra/core` will crash with `ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND`

### Link to Minimal Reproducible Example

Workaround in place.

### Expected Behavior

Server routes importing @mastra/core should work on Vercel with pnpm's default settings without requiring node-linker=hoisted.

### Environment Information

```block
Binaries:
    Node: 22.20.0
    npm: 10.9.3
    pnpm: 10.32.1
```

### Verification

- [x] I have searched the existing issues to make sure this is not a duplicate
- [x] I have included sufficient information for the team to reproduce and understand the issue

body:
### Describe the Bug

When a parent workflow run is created with resourceId, nested child workflows do not pass that resourceId through to persistWorkflowSnapshot. This causes child workflow snapshots/runs to lose tenant/resource association.
https://github.com/mastra-ai/mastra/blob/067b2250b5aaba9334d06ca3eaa1f230942ce5b3/packages/core/src/workflows/workflow.ts#L2277

### Steps To Reproduce

```
const child = createWorkflow({...}).then(childStep)
child.commit()

const parent = createWorkflow({...}).then(child)
parent.commit()

const run = await parent.createRun({
  runId: 'run-1',
  resourceId: 'workspace-1',
})

await run.start({ inputData: { ok: true } })

```
Observed result:

parent snapshot calls include resourceId: 'workspace-1'
child snapshot calls have resourceId: undefined

### Link to Minimal Reproducible Example

https://github.com/amitava82/mastra-demo

### Expected Behavior

If a parent workflow is started with:

```
const run = await workflow.createRun({ runId: 'run-1', resourceId: 'workspace-1' })
```
then nested workflows invoked inside that workflow should also persist snapshots with resourceId: 'workspace-1'.


### Environment Information

```block
@mastra/core: 1.24.1
```

### Verification

- [x] I have searched the existing issues to make sure this is not a duplicate
- [x] I have included sufficient information for the team to reproduce and understand the issue

body:
### Describe the Bug

When Observational Memory (OM) is enabled, client-side tool (tool without `execute` function) execution results are lost on the next request. The agent cannot see the tool result and acts as if it never arrived.

OM's `filterObservedMessages` removes messages that have been "observed", but has no awareness of tool-call / tool-result pairing. On the second request (when the client sends back the tool result), OM removes the tool-call message because its ID is in `observedMessageIds`, leaving the tool-result orphaned — the LLM sees a result without the corresponding call.

This differs from #14282 which only fixed **provider-executed tools** (e.g. Anthropic `web_search`) by deferring observation when `state: 'call'` is detected mid-stream. Client-side tools return in a separate HTTP request where the call already has `state: 'result'`, so that guard doesn't help.


### Steps To Reproduce

1. Create an agent with OM enabled and a client-side tool:

```ts
const agent = new Agent({
  name: 'test-agent',
  instructions: 'You are a helpful assistant.',
  model: openai('gpt-4o'),
  tools: {
    myTool: createTool({
      id: 'my-tool',
      description: 'A client-side tool',
      inputSchema: z.object({ query: z.string() }),
      // no execute → client-side tool
    }),
  },
  memory: new Memory({
    options: { observationalMemory: { enabled: true } },
  }),
});
```

2. Start a conversation. The agent generates a tool call (`state: 'call'`) and returns it to the client.

3. Client executes the tool locally and sends the result back in a new request (messages include the tool-call + tool-result).

4. **Expected**: Agent sees the tool result and continues the conversation.

5. **Actual**: Agent does not see the tool result — it's effectively lost due to OM filtering.

### Link to Minimal Reproducible Example

/

### Expected Behavior

The agent should see the client-side tool result and continue the conversation normally. `filterObservedMessages` (`packages/memory/src/processors/observational-memory/message-utils.ts`) should check for tool-call/tool-result pair integrity before removing messages. If a message containing a `tool-invocation` part is about to be removed, the filter should verify that no remaining message has a `tool-result` part referencing the same `toolCallId`. If an orphaned result would be created, either keep the tool-call message or remove both.

**Workaround**: Converting client-side tools to server-side tools (adding an `execute` function) avoids this, since results are resolved within the same request's agentic loop before OM's cross-request filtering kicks in.


### Environment Information

```block
- `@mastra/core`: >= 1.22.0
- `@mastra/memory`: >= 1.13.0
```

### Verification

- [x] I have searched the existing issues to make sure this is not a duplicate
- [x] I have included sufficient information for the team to reproduce and understand the issue

body:
## Bug Description

`prepareCallSettings()` in `packages/core/src/core/prompt/prepare-call-settings.ts` defaults `temperature` to `0` when not explicitly set:

```typescript
// TODO v5 remove default 0 for temperature
temperature: temperature != null ? temperature : 0,
```

This causes **hard failures** with models that restrict temperature values. For example, Moonshot's **Kimi K2.5** only accepts `temperature=1` and rejects any other value with:

```
400 Bad Request: "invalid temperature: only 1 is allowed for this model"
```

Since most users don't explicitly set temperature (expecting the model's own default), every `agent.stream()` / `agent.generate()` call silently forces `temperature=0`, which breaks these models.

## Reproduction

```typescript
import { Agent } from '@mastra/core/agent';
import { createOpenAI } from '@ai-sdk/openai';

const provider = createOpenAI({
  baseURL: 'https://api.moonshot.cn/v1',
  apiKey: '...',
});

const agent = new Agent({
  name: 'test',
  model: provider.chat('kimi-k2-5'),
  instructions: 'You are a helpful assistant.',
});

// Fails: 400 "invalid temperature: only 1 is allowed for this model"
const result = await agent.stream('Hello');
```

## Expected Behavior

When `temperature` is not set by the user, it should be `undefined` so that the AI SDK provider omits it from the request body, letting the model use its own default.

## Proposed Fix

One-line change — the TODO comment already exists in the code:

```diff
- // TODO v5 remove default 0 for temperature
- temperature: temperature != null ? temperature : 0,
+ temperature,
```

This is safe because:
- AI SDK providers (`@ai-sdk/openai`, etc.) correctly strip `undefined` fields from requests
- Users who explicitly set `temperature: 0` still get deterministic behavior
- Only the "didn't set anything" case changes: from forced `0` to model default
- Aligns with how every other parameter (`topP`, `topK`, `presencePenalty`, etc.) is already handled in the same function — none of them have a forced default

## Current Workaround

We intercept the fetch call and strip `temperature: 0` from the request body before sending, which is fragile and shouldn't be necessary.

## Environment

- `@mastra/core`: 1.22.0
- Model: Kimi K2.5 (Moonshot AI) via `@ai-sdk/openai` compatible provider

I'm happy to submit a PR for this if the team is open to it — it's a one-line change.

comments.body:
Thank you for reporting this issue! We have labeled it for trio-tb and we will look into it as soon as possible.

If you're experiencing an error, please provide a [minimal reproducible example](https://github.com/mastra-ai/mastra/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#minimal-reproduction) whenever possible to help us resolve it quickly.

## Root cause investigation

After digging deeper, the `prepareCallSettings` function with `temperature ?? 0` is **not Mastra's own code** — it's bundled from `ai@4.3.19` (Vercel AI SDK v4) during `@mastra/core`'s build process.

The current AI SDK v6 (`ai@6.x`) has already removed this default:

```typescript
// ai@6.x — fixed, no default
return { temperature, ... };
```

But `@mastra/core` bundles the older `ai@4.3.19` via `@internal/ai-sdk-v4`, which still has:

```typescript
// ai@4.3.19 — hardcoded default
temperature: temperature != null ? temperature : 0,
```

### Possible fixes

1. **Upgrade the bundled `ai` version** in `@internal/ai-sdk-v4` to a version that doesn't default temperature to 0
2. **Patch the `prepareCallSettings` call** in Mastra's own code to explicitly pass `temperature: undefined` in a way that survives the bundled SDK's default
3. **Add a post-processing step** in the Agent's `stream()`/`generate()` methods to strip `temperature: 0` when the user didn't explicitly set it

Option 1 seems cleanest. Happy to help with a PR if you can point me to the right approach for updating the internal AI SDK bundle.

## Root Cause Update

After deeper investigation, the `temperature ?? 0` default is **not in Mastra's own source code** — it comes from the vendored AI SDK v4 dependency:

**Dependency chain:**
```
packages/core/src/llm/model/model.ts
  → imports from @internal/ai-sdk-v4 (packages/_vendored/ai_v4)
    → re-exports from ai@^4.3.19
      → ai/core/prompt/prepare-call-settings.ts contains:
        temperature: temperature != null ? temperature : 0  // TODO v5 remove default 0 for temperature
```

**Key findings:**
- `ai@4.3.19` is the **last v4 release** — this will never be fixed in v4
- Vercel AI SDK v5/v6 already removed this default (just passes `temperature` through as-is)
- The TODO comment in the AI SDK v4 source confirms this was always known tech debt
- Since `@internal/ai-sdk-v4` is vendored via `packages/_vendored/ai_v4`, the Mastra team controls the bundling

**Possible fix approaches:**
1. **pnpm patch** on `ai@4.3.19` to remove the `?? 0` fallback
2. **Post-build transform** in the vendored package's tsup config to strip the default
3. **Migrate to AI SDK v5/v6** for `@internal/ai-sdk-v4` (if feasible)

I'll leave the implementation approach to the team since it involves internal vendored dependency management. Happy to help with a PR if you can point me to the preferred approach.
@hikariming we support v4, v5, and v6, does this run for all versions or just v4? (haven't looked myself)

body:
### Describe the Bug

AgentConfig.tools accepts an invalid value that should fail type checking. In a static tools map,
  each entry should already be a tool object, but TypeScript currently allows a nested resolver
  function like myTool: () => realTool.

  That compiles without error, even though it is not a valid static tool definition. The bad value
  then survives to runtime, where agent.listTools() crashes instead of the mistake being caught by
  TypeScript.

### Steps To Reproduce

1. Clone the reproduction repo: https://github.com/abrahamcl/mastra-bug-agent-tools

## Reproduction

```sh
npm install
npx tsc --noEmit
```

Expected: TypeScript reports an error for `myTool: () => realTool`.

Actual: `tsc` exits successfully with no type error.

To show that an actual runtime crash occurs when using the invalid config, run:

```sh
npm run test
```

### Link to Minimal Reproducible Example

https://github.com/abrahamcl/mastra-bug-agent-tools

### Expected Behavior

Typescript should flag an invalid tool value

### Environment Information

```block
System:
    OS: macOS 26.3
    CPU: (8) arm64 Apple M3
    Memory: 128.28 MB / 24.00 GB
    Shell: 5.9 - /bin/zsh
  Binaries:
    Node: 25.8.1 - /opt/homebrew/bin/node
    Yarn: 1.22.22 - /opt/homebrew/bin/yarn
    npm: 11.11.0 - /opt/homebrew/bin/npm
    pnpm: 10.32.1 - /opt/homebrew/bin/pnpm
    bun: 1.3.10 - /opt/homebrew/bin/bun
  Browsers:
    Chrome: 146.0.7680.165
    Safari: 26.3
  npmPackages:
    @mastra/core: ^1.24.1 => 1.24.1 
    @types/node: ^25.5.2 => 25.5.2 
    mastra: ^1.5.0 => 1.5.0 
    typescript: ^6.0.2 => 6.0.2 
    vitest: ^4.1.4 => 4.1.4 
    zod: ^4.3.6 => 4.3.6 
  npmGlobalPackages:
    @openai/codex: 0.75.0
    codex: 0.2.3
    httpyac: 6.16.7
    mint: 4.2.60
    npm: 11.11.0
```

- [x] I have searched the existing issues to make sure this is not a duplicate
- [x] I have included sufficient information for the team to reproduce and understand the issue

body:
This issue was created from Discord post 1491822366936797327:

[![Open in Discord](https://img.shields.io/badge/Open_in_Discord-5865F2?style=for-the-badge&logo=discord&logoColor=white)](https://discord.com/channels/1309558646228779139/1491822366936797327)

Hi everyone! 👋

I’m currently exploring Mastra to evaluate whether it fits my use case. First of all, great job — the editor experience is really nice 👍
That said, while building some demos, I ran into a few behaviors that felt a bit unexpected, especially around agents and prompts.
From my perspective, the idea of stored agents is very powerful. However, right now it feels like there’s a bit of a mix between programmatic agents and stored/editor-managed agents:

You can create an agent programmatically and then edit its prompt in the editor.
Once edited, the prompt becomes a stored entity (Prompt block), and the agent itself is also stored in the database.
After this point, if you try to modify the prompt again in code, it no longer has any effect — the “programmatic” definition seems to be overridden or locked.

This makes it hard to reason about the source of truth for an agent. Maybe it would help to more clearly separate stored agents vs purely programmatic agents, or at least make the lifecycle and precedence rules more explicit.
I also experimented with creating prompts and agents via mastra.getEditor, but it doesn’t feel like there’s a complete end‑to‑end flow yet. For example, listAgents doesn’t return stored agents (which might be a bug?).

Overall, I’m really enjoying Mastra and I love the direction it’s going ❤️
Thanks a lot for the amazing work, and happy to discuss or clarify anything further!

title: How Should Programmatic and Stored Agents Coexist?

body:
### Problem Statement

I noticed that we can't create a tool with a conditional execution depending/suspended/waiting for multiple questions and their replies.

---

**Case:**

For example, you create a tool "Insert a document in a table", and it takes multiple parameters.  

---

**Currently** : 

As you set multiple parameters, the LLM will read the context and try to find the values to these parameters, and if they are not set/obvious reading the context, it currently asks in a natural language a little message to ask for the user to fill the different parameters. The main issue is that "natural language" conversation message makes mastra to immediately leave the tool (it does not suspend it) and this message is just an "arbitrary" generated text with always a different formatting (sometimes with tirets/bullet points, sometimes with some sentences asking for the different parameters in a natural language, etc). 
To avoid such behavior, we created a tool "Ask 1 or several questions to the user", that we made so it enriches the requestContext and append in it a predictable object of the questions, So my frontend always get the questions in a predictable format and the backend waits for the replies. Indeed, in the tool, we suspend the tool waiting for the user message containing the replies. This seems to work but looks overkill and the context becomes a bit not predictable and big.

Note 1 : Doing a single suspended tool call allow some tokens economy. Because we avoid to produce a first response with the questions in the context history (because the first suspend does not enrich context with questions, and the history just does  "user : 'Add a document', tool: 'Got the answers of the user with Q1: R1, Q2: R2, Q3: R3', ..." and then goes on.  If not suspended to the replies, it would be :  "user: 'Add a document', tool: 'Please answer the questions Q1 Q2 Q3', user: 'My answers are R1, R2, R3', ..."  and then goes on.

Note 2: Cf bottom of this message, about "another approach".

---

**What I would like to do :**

Ideally, it would be much better to have a kind of "requireApproval" for a tool, but based on parameters and that would always build a consistent Record object of the definition of the questions returned to the frontend. For example : 

```typescript
const insertTool = createTool({
  id: 'insert-record',
  description: 'Insert a record',
  inputSchema: z.object({ name: z.string(), age: z.number() }),
  outputSchema: z.object({ inserted_id: z.boolean() }),
  requiredParamsForm: true, // Says the params are needed (and so are returned in the response right now in a predictable format json key in the chunk response)
  // ...
```
It would response a response to the user, with the formatted n-questions `[ {type: "string", name: "name"}, {type: "number", name: "age"}, ...]`

And later, after receiving the payload of the user in a valid `{ "answers": [ {name: "name", value: "John" ... ]}`
```typescript
for await (const chunk of stream.fullStream) {
  if (chunk.type === 'tool-call-replied') {
    const approved = await agent.fillForm({ runId: stream.runId, replies: chunk.replies }) // Replies comes from the front as the expected json format.
    for await (const c of approved.textStream) process.stdout.write(c)
  }
}
```

--- 

**Another approach:**

We could also NOT have a tool "Ask one or several questions", and could just create a prompt system rule with: "If some questions are needed to execute a tool that are requested but have some required parameters, ask for the user to reply a set of questions (1 or several questions) in a form in the given format: { questions: [ { ... }, ....] }.

This approach actually works, but, again, it adds useless context and is a bit painful for an obvious feature as the one described here.

### Proposed Solution

The "ideal" solution as explained in the problem.

### Component

Tools

### Alternatives Considered

_No response_

### Example Use Case

The example given in the problem.

### Additional Context

_No response_

### Verification

- [x] I have searched the existing issues to make sure this is not a duplicate
- [x] I have provided sufficient context for the team to understand the request

body:
We just shipped a small sample on the Assay side that consumes a frozen Mastra scorer / experiment-item result as external evidence:

https://github.com/Rul1an/assay/tree/main/examples/mastra-scorer-evidence

The reason for the sample is pretty narrow: we wanted to test the smallest honest Mastra reliability surface an external evidence consumer could ingest without collapsing back into traces, Studio metrics, or dashboard semantics.

So we kept the shape intentionally small:
- scorer name
- score
- outcome
- dataset version ref
- item ref
- target type
- timestamp

We are not treating that artifact as Mastra truth, and we are not assuming the checked-in fixture shape is a stable wire contract.

The question is mainly about seam choice:

If an external evidence consumer wants the smallest honest Mastra reliability surface, is a bounded scorer / experiment-item result roughly the right place to start, or is there a thinner scorer result surface you would rather point them at?

If we are aiming at the wrong layer, happy to adjust the sample.

comments.body:
Thank you for reporting this issue! We have labeled it for trio-tracery and we will look into it as soon as possible.

If you're experiencing an error, please provide a [minimal reproducible example](https://github.com/mastra-ai/mastra/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#minimal-reproduction) whenever possible to help us resolve it quickly.

Hey @Rul1an — to make sure I’m reading this right: you’re asking whether the scorer / experiment-item result is the right integration point for an external consumer that needs a minimal reliability signal.

A heads up that this may not be the right surface to build against. The scorer itself is just a score definition — it doesn’t carry result data. And experiment-item results aren’t likely to be the place where scored output lives going forward. Score results will live in the observability scores table. 

If you’re looking for the thinnest honest surface for external consumption, that’s probably where you want to be looking.

Can you share a bit more about what Assay is doing with this data downstream? Would help us understand whether you’re pointed at the right thing.

Thanks, this is very helpful.

Yes, that’s the question we’re trying to answer: what is the smallest honest Mastra surface an external consumer could ingest as a bounded reliability signal.

What Assay does downstream is pretty narrow. We treat imported artifacts as external evidence, not as framework truth. In practice that means:
- carry a small upstream score signal into a reviewable evidence plane
- keep freshness and provenance attached
- avoid inheriting traces, dashboard semantics, or runtime correctness as truth

So if score results are moving to the observability scores table, that sounds like the better seam for us than scorer definitions or experiment-item results.

In that case we’d likely re-cut the sample around one bounded score record, probably something closer to:
- scorer identifier
- score value
- target reference
- timestamp
- optional short reason reference

and keep trace, log, and broader observability payloads out of v1.

To make this real on our side, we can switch from the current frozen scorer / experiment-item sample to a tiny Mastra app with scoring enabled, persist one actual score result to storage, and rebuild the sample from that row-level shape instead.

If that direction matches where Mastra is going, is a single score record from the observability scores store roughly the right place to start for an external consumer, or is there an even thinner score-result surface you’d rather point us at?

Quick update from our side: we’ve now merged the Assay-side recut and rebuilt the sample around the bounded exporter / score-event seam you pointed us to.

https://github.com/Rul1an/assay/tree/main/examples/mastra-score-event-evidence

We kept it intentionally small and intentionally honest. In particular, we are not treating the richer frozen sample shape as proof that the live exporter callback already arrives that way today. It is still pre-proof on the live callback path, and the next step for us is the boring but important one: capture one real callback and check the sample against that.

That steer was really helpful, trouwens. If there is one field here you’d drop or rename to keep the sample even closer to the exporter payload as it exists today, happy to tighten it a bit more. Otherwise we’ll treat this as the right direction and do the live capture next.

Quick update from our side: we merged the follow-up and tightened the sample another notch around the seam you pointed us to.

So it is now explicitly `ScoreEvent`-first, `addScoreToTrace(...)` is only kept as legacy / migration context, and we added one bounded slot for the upcoming `ScoreId` anchor as well.

https://github.com/Rul1an/assay/tree/main/examples/mastra-score-event-evidence

We still are not treating that as live callback proof yet. The next boring but important step for us is still to capture one real `onScoreEvent` and check the frozen sample against it.

That extra nudge was exactly right, thanks.

Small follow-up from our side: we went ahead and did the boring proof step and captured one real local `onScoreEvent` from a minimal Mastra harness, then checked our frozen Assay sample against it.

The useful bit is pretty clear now: the forward `onScoreEvent` path is definitely live in a modern local run. In that same run we also still saw one legacy `addScoreToTrace(...)` call, so for now we’re treating that as migration / co-fire context rather than fully gone-gone.

The first real callback was also thinner than our earlier frozen sample, which was actually helpful. We saw `timestamp`, `traceId`, `spanId`, `scorerId`, `scoreSource`, `score`, `scoreTraceId`, `correlationContext`, and `metadata`. We did not see `scorerName`, `reason`, top-level `targetEntityType`, `scoreId`, or one native upstream `targetRef` field in that run.

So we narrowed the sample again instead of pretending the richer shape was already proven:

https://github.com/Rul1an/assay/pull/1090

This still does not claim one local run defines a universal Mastra export contract, but it does close the immediate proof gap for our sample lane in a way that feels a lot more eerlijk.

If there’s one field in that live callback set that you’d especially expect external consumers to lean on next, we’re happy to keep the sample tight around that.

When using a harness with a PostgresStore, I get the following errors for basically every tool call:

updateToolInvocation: no matching tool call found for toolCallId=...

When I replace the `PostgresStore` with a `LibSQLStore`, everything works perfectly as expected.

Reproduction help:

```
const storage = new PostgresStore({
  id: 'mastra-postgres-storage',
  host: 'localhost',
  port: 5432,
  database: 'mastra',
  user: 'mastra',
  password: '...',
  ssl: false,
});
```
- I am initializing mastra with that storage, and the harness with that storage. The harness also has observational memory configured.
- Now, lots of tools (resumed tools, but also simple mastra workspace tools straight-up) show this warning.

Versions of relevant packages:
```
@mastra/core': 1.24.0
@mastra/libsql': 1.8.0
@mastra/memory': 1.15.0
@mastra/pg': 1.9.0
mastra: 1.4.1
```

The difference in working perfectly vs not working between LibSQL and Postgres makes me think this is unintended behaviour in the mastra/pg package, instead of some user error on my end, but let me know if you need more info.

body:
This issue was created from Discord post 1490893421521539145:

[![Open in Discord](https://img.shields.io/badge/Open_in_Discord-5865F2?style=for-the-badge&logo=discord&logoColor=white)](https://discord.com/channels/1309558646228779139/1490893421521539145)

PR #13418 ("strip OpenAI reasoning parts from LLM input to prevent item pairing errors") introduced reasoning stripping in `sanitizeV5UIMessages` to fix memory-replay pairing errors. However, I think the fix was too aggressive: it seems to strip reasoning parts from the **entire** message list without distinguishing between:

1. **Current-run step results** - reasoning from step N that must reach step N+1 for reasoning continuity (should be kept, the issue I think I'm having)
2. **Memory-replayed items from prior conversation turns** - which may have broken pairing and cause API rejections (correct to strip)

What happens now: encrypted reasoning content from step N is never sent back to the model on step N+1 in multi-step agent loops. The model loses its chain-of-thought between tool-calling steps and must reconstruct its plan from scratch, leading to degraded instruction-following (e.g., producing text + tool calls in the same output instead of sequentially - not to be confused with preamble).

The core problem seems to be that `sanitizeV5UIMessages` operates on a flat UI message list and can't distinguish "came from memory replay" from "came from the current agent run."

I've validated the same issue for me in both `@mastra/core` 1.21.0 and 1.22.0.

comments.body:
Thank you for reporting this issue! We have labeled it for trio-tnt and we will look into it as soon as possible.

If you're experiencing an error, please provide a [minimal reproducible example](https://github.com/mastra-ai/mastra/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#minimal-reproduction) whenever possible to help us resolve it quickly.

Adding the rest of the message I included in Discord to make sure it's not missed here.

**My Specific Environment**
- Model: `openai/gpt-5-mini` via Responses API
- `store: false` (don't store conversations in OpenAI infra)
- `include: ['reasoning.encrypted_content']`
- Multi-step agent loop with tool calls (`agent.stream()` with `savePerStep: true`)

The core issues I've been having lately which took me a while to track down why this behavior was occurring:

1. **Reasoning continuity broken**: The model cannot refer to its previous reasoning traces between tool-calling steps. OpenAI's GPT-5 prompting guide specifically recommends passing encrypted reasoning for "conserving CoT tokens and eliminating the need to reconstruct a plan from scratch after each tool call."

2. **Degraded model behavior**: Without reasoning continuity, the model intermittently produces text and tool calls in the same generation step instead of sequentially. This causes agent loops, duplicate tool executions, and fabricated data (agent seeing it's own response but inferring it like a user message).

3. **Item IDs lost**: The `openai` metadata stripping also removes `itemId` from text and tool-call parts, so `function_call` items in the Responses API input lack `id` fields.

**Root cause** (patching this locally addresses the issue for me at least)

In `sanitizeV5UIMessages` (lines 107 - 246 of `output-converter`), when `filterIncompleteToolCalls` is `true`:

```typescript
const hasOpenAIReasoning = filterIncompleteToolCalls && m.parts.some(
  (p) => p.type === "reasoning" && "providerMetadata" in p && p.providerMetadata
    && typeof p.providerMetadata === "object" && "openai" in p.providerMetadata
);

const safeParts = m.parts.filter((p) => {
  // ...
  if (p.type === "reasoning" && hasOpenAIReasoning) {
    return false;  // ← ALL reasoning parts stripped
  }
```

Additionally, lines ~203 - 213 strip the `openai` key from `providerMetadata` on ALL remaining parts, removing `itemId` values needed for the Responses API.

`llmPrompt()` (lines 373 - 499 in `message-list.ts` calls `sanitizeV5UIMessages` with `filterIncompleteToolCalls = true` via `convertAIV5UIToModelMessages` (alias for `aiV5UIMessagesToAIV5ModelMessages`):

```typescript
const modelMessages = aiV5UIMessagesToAIV5ModelMessages(this.all.aiV5.ui(), this.messages, true);
```

So every agent loop prompt has reasoning stripped.

**Evidence** (particular to my use, but useful to convey the difference):

Debug traces comparing API requests to OpenAI before and after fix:

**Before fix** (step 1 API request):
```
input items: 4
reasoning items: 0 (NONE)
function_call items with ids: 0
```

**After fix** (step 1 API request):
```
input items: 15
reasoning items: 3 (with encrypted: 3)
```

Before fix: mixed text+tool steps occurred on 50%+ of conversations within 4 messages.
After fix: 9 messages, 17 iterations, zero mixed text+tool steps. (and continuing to be stable in new threads)

**Suggested fix**

The stripping logic from PR #13418 is necessary for certain edge cases (duplicate consecutive reasoning items in memory replay, Gemini empty reasoning parts from #12980), but it's too broad.

**What's known about the pairing error:**

From [OpenAI community thread 1280238](https://community.openai.com/t/responses-api-error-with-type-reasoning-provided-without-its-required-following-item/1280238) (verbatim):
- OP `awesie`: *"It really looks like the Responses API thinks that there are two reasoning outputs in a row."* The failing pattern they observed was `[reasoning, reasoning, function_call]` (two consecutive reasoning items).
- `Fix_AI`: *"If you remove any 'id' properties from items in the input list before sending it, that seems to get rid of all the reasoning errors."*

From [openai-python #2561](https://github.com/openai/openai-python/issues/2561): the error reliably triggers with multi-agent handoffs + Code Interpreter, not with every multi-step reasoning scenario.

From PR #13418's own analysis: the pairing error on the OpenAI side is linked to `item_reference` sending when `store: true` and IDs don't resolve properly, not to inline reasoning content.

**What remains unverified:** OpenAI does not seem to have documentation on the exact pairing rules for reasoning items with `encrypted_content`. My confidence that the patched behavior is correct comes from empirical testing. If there's a subtle edge case in memory replay scenarios that still needs protection, Option B below is the safer path.

**Two possible approaches:**

**Option A: Keep encrypted reasoning that's followed by valid items (simplest):** Reasoning parts that carry `encrypted_content` and appear in their natural position in the message flow are valid Responses API input. The pairing error doesn't apply to them. This is the workaround below.

**Option B: Track message source:** Mark memory-replayed messages distinctly from current-run messages, and only strip reasoning from the former. More invasive but semantically cleaner. This should handle both the current-run continuity case and the memory-replay edge case explicitly.

**Workaround (Option A)** (what we have patched locally)

Patch `sanitizeV5UIMessages` to keep reasoning parts that carry `encrypted_content`:

```typescript
if (p.type === "reasoning" && hasOpenAIReasoning) {
  // Keep reasoning parts with encrypted content for store:false reasoning continuity
  if (p.providerMetadata?.openai?.reasoningEncryptedContent) {
    return true;
  }
  return false;
}
```

And skip stripping `openai` metadata from those parts:

```typescript
const isEncryptedReasoning = part.type === "reasoning"
  && part.providerMetadata?.openai?.reasoningEncryptedContent;
if (!isEncryptedReasoning) {
  // existing openai metadata stripping logic
}
```

title: json-render

body:
## Problem

Three methods in `@mastra/client-js` Vector resource have TypeScript return types that don't match what the server actually returns. This means users get **no TypeScript errors** but hit **runtime failures** when they trust the SDK types.

## Mismatches

| Method | SDK declares | Server returns |
|---|---|---|
| `getIndexes()` | `Promise<{ indexes: string[] }>` | `string[]` (unwrapped array) |
| `upsert()` | `Promise<string[]>` | `{ ids: string[] }` (wrapped object) |
| `query()` | `Promise<QueryVectorResponse>` | `QueryResult[]` (raw array) |

## Where the code lives

- **SDK types:** `client-sdks/client-js/src/resources/vector.ts` (methods: `getIndexes`, `upsert`, `query`)
- **Server handlers:** `packages/server/src/server/handlers/vector.ts` (routes: `LIST_INDEXES_ROUTE`, `UPSERT_VECTORS_ROUTE`, `QUERY_VECTORS_ROUTE`)

## Impact

Users writing TypeScript get wrong autocomplete and no type errors, but their code breaks at runtime:

```typescript
const response = await vector.getIndexes();
console.log(response.indexes); // 💥 undefined — response is actually string[]
```

## Fix options

Either fix the SDK types to match the server, or fix the server to match the SDK types. Both are breaking changes and need team discussion on which side to align.

## Discovered in

PR #13081 — client-js E2E tests surfaced this while testing the full MastraClient → Server → Storage stack.

title: fix(client-js): Vector SDK return types don't match server responses

body:
### Describe the Bug

When a coordinator agent (an agent with sub-agents via the agents property) has observationalMemory: true, the coordinator's text response is generated by the LLM but never persisted to the database. Only the tool invocations (sub-agent delegations) and data-om-status markers are saved.

### Steps To Reproduce

Create a coordinator agent with sub-agents and observationalMemory: true:

```
const coordinator = new Agent({
  id: 'coordinator',
  instructions: '...',
  model: 'google/gemini-3-flash-preview',
  agents: { subAgent },
  memory: new Memory({
    storage: pgStore,
    options: {
      observationalMemory: true,
      workingMemory: { enabled: true, schema: z.object({ ... }) },
    },
  }),
});
```

Send a message that causes the coordinator to delegate to a sub-agent and then respond with text.
Query the stored messages for the coordinator's thread:

```
SELECT id, role, content::text
FROM mastra.mastra_messages
WHERE thread_id = '<thread_id>' AND role = 'assistant';
```
Expected: The assistant message contains both tool-invocation parts AND a text part with the coordinator's response.

[mastra_messages_rows.json](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26490078/mastra_messages_rows.json)

Actual: The assistant message contains only data-om-status, tool-invocation, and step-start parts. No text part is stored. The content top-level field is also missing.

**Root cause analysis:**

The issue is in ObservationalMemoryProcessor's interaction with ObservationTurn during multi-step LLM responses.

When the coordinator's LLM response spans multiple steps (step 0: tool call → step 1: text after tool result), the following occurs:

Step 0: LLM generates a tool call. Assistant message M1 is created in the MessageList with [tool-invocation] parts, tracked in the "response" source bucket.

Step 1 processInputStep: Because stepNumber > 0, the processor calls messageList.clear.response.db(), which:

Returns M1 from the "response" bucket
Persists M1 to the database (with only tool invocations, no text yet)
Moves M1 to the "memory" source bucket via messageList.add(msg, "memory")
Step 1 execution: The LLM generates the text response. The text parts are appended to M1 in-memory, but M1 is now in the "memory" bucket, not "response".

turn.end(): Checks only messageList.get.response.db() and messageList.get.input.db() for unsaved messages. The "response" bucket is empty (M1 was moved to "memory" in step 2). Nothing is re-saved. The text generated in step 1 is lost.

The database retains the version of M1 from step 2 — containing tool invocations and OM status markers but no text.

Verification: Langfuse traces confirm the coordinator LLM does generate text (visible in the trace output and as chunk: 'text' spans under step 1). The text is produced but not persisted.

Why it works without observational memory: When observationalMemory is disabled (the default — new Memory() without the option), the ObservationalMemoryProcessor is not registered. Without it, there is no step-level message clearing, so all parts (tool invocations + text) remain in the "response" bucket and are saved together at the end.

Workaround: Set observationalMemory: false in the coordinator agent's memory config.

Suggested fix: ObservationTurn.end() should detect messages in the "memory" bucket that have been modified since their last persist (e.g., by comparing part counts or a dirty flag) and re-save them. Alternatively, the step transition in processInputStep could avoid clearing the response bucket until the full turn is complete.

### Link to Minimal Reproducible Example

https://github.com/00md13/mastra-memory-issue

### Expected Behavior

The assistant message contains both tool-invocation parts AND a text part with the coordinator's response.

### Environment Information

```block
@mastra/core: 1.21.0
@mastra/memory: 1.13.0
@mastra/pg: 1.8.5
@mastra/server: 1.21.0
```

### Verification

- [x] I have searched the existing issues to make sure this is not a duplicate
- [x] I have included sufficient information for the team to reproduce and understand the issue

comments.body:
Thank you for reporting this issue! We have labeled it for trio-tb and we will look into it as soon as possible.

If you're experiencing an error, please provide a [minimal reproducible example](https://github.com/mastra-ai/mastra/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#minimal-reproduction) whenever possible to help us resolve it quickly.

Hey @00md13 This was fixed in a later release (between 1.13.0 and 1.14.0). The MessageList.addOne() merge path now calls pushMessageToSource(latestMessage, 'response') which puts the modified message back in the response bucket, so turn.end() correctly finds and persists it. Please upgrade @mastra/memory to 1.14.0+.

Hey @mastra_ai @tylbar
 — love your Observational Memory work. Hit an interesting edge case: when observations grow large enough that the reflector can't compress below the threshold even at MAX_COMPRESSION_LEVEL, it retries indefinitely rather than accepting the best result. 

The fix we'd love to see: accept best result after exhausting retries, and optionally a priority-based injection budget (newest + 🔴 items first) so OM stays useful even when the full sheet is large.

Happy to PR either or both if useful!
https://x.com/RickRossTN/status/2040533943242621267?s=20

title: Nested Agent data-tool-agent event does not include ordered message parts

body:
This issue was created from Discord post 1489894903981936753:

[![Open in Discord](https://img.shields.io/badge/Open_in_Discord-5865F2?style=for-the-badge&logo=discord&logoColor=white)](https://discord.com/channels/1309558646228779139/1489894903981936753)

Hey team, found a message persistence bug that I believe is in `@mastra/core`'s `ProcessorRunner.applyMessagesToMessageList`.

**TL;DR:** In multi-step agent executions (router delegates to sub-agent), the final assistant response is lost from memory when Observational Memory's token threshold isn't reached. The message streams fine but disappears on refresh.

The issue: when a `processOutputStep` processor returns messages unchanged, `applyMessagesToMessageList` does an unconditional remove-and-re-add of every message. This corrupts the `newResponseMessages` Set tracking when there's a single merged assistant message spanning both steps. `response.db()` returns empty after the re-add.

When OM's token threshold IS reached (large delegation results), `rotateResponseMessageId` fires, step 2 gets a new ID, creates separate messages — and those survive the re-add. So the bug only manifests with small/empty delegation results.

Disabling OM on the router eliminates it. 

Fix suggestion: skip the remove/re-add in `applyMessagesToMessageList` when the message object reference is already in the messageList (same ref = no-op, processor mutated in-place or returned unchanged).

1. Create a router agent with `observationalMemory: { enabled: true }` and a `processOutputStep` processor
2. Configure a sub-agent with tools (search, etc.)
3. Ask a question that triggers delegation where the sub-agent returns a **small** result (few tokens — e.g., empty search results, or a small structured output)
4. The router streams the full response correctly
5. Refresh the page — **the final assistant text is gone**
6. Repeat with a query that produces a **large** delegation result (many tokens) — it works

**I am currently testing a solution and testing the implementation, can open a PR once testing is finished. ** and after dane 🐶  finishes the issue report (I do not have time to create a reproducable repo at the moment & working directly with forked mastra to test interally)

title: 3-level sub-agent delegation: no progressive streaming to client

comments.body:
Thank you for reporting this issue! We have labeled it for trio-tb and we will look into it as soon as possible.

If you're experiencing an error, please provide a [minimal reproducible example](https://github.com/mastra-ai/mastra/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#minimal-reproduction) whenever possible to help us resolve it quickly.
Hi, I'd like to investigate and fix this. I'll dig into how providerOptions are passed when a supervisor agent invokes sub-agents and report back.

I traced the execution path. In map-results-step.ts, ...options (which is InnerAgentExecutionOptions) is spread into ModelLoopStreamArgs, which means the parent agent's providerOptions (including reasoningEffort: 'none') flows into the sub-agent's LLM execution. The sub-agent's own defaultOptions.providerOptions may be getting overridden by the parent's context. Confirming the exact merge order before opening a PR.

@Aayush-engineer thank you for getting started with this - I've asked the original contributor on GitHub to post a reproduction of his code here, so that we have a very clear understanding of how he is structuring the relationship between the supervisor and the sub-agents. It's a pretty core piece of functionality, so we want to make sure that your analysis and his use-case match up so that the PR can be clear, and so that our engineering team can have all the context it needs for reviewing it.

Dependencies:

@ai-sdk/azure: 3.0.50
@mastra/core": 1.20.0
ai: 6.0.142
mastra: 1.3.18

Model:

gpt-5-mini via Azure OpenAI

What I want to achieve:

We have a supervisor agent, its only task is to delegate the user request to correct sub-agent and handle basic user interaction.

Now the main user experience comes here, as a user if I am justing "Hey", Agent is still taking roughly 6-7 seconds to respond. There are 2 main reasons for this latency:

  • Network latency while making request to and fro Azure and our servers
  • Model's internal thinking

Out of this, the second point is an easiest fix for me because OpenAI gives us a way to stop reasoning for a model to have lowest latency for execution-heavy tasks such as extraction, routing, or simple transforms.

So to test this out, I gave Supervisor Agent the same gpt-5-mini model with reasoningEffort set to none. It worked well. I received responses for simple queries which doesn't require delegation in 1.5-2 seconds.

This Supervisor Agent has a sub-agent called RAG and this basically handles the QAs over a vast knowledgebase. This RAG agent is supposed to reason on the user query and iterate over the tools it has in order to achieve the factually correct answer. (Refer RAG Agent architecture)

RAG Agent architecture:

I have a sub-agent which has only task to get correct information from the knowledegbase. For doing so it has four tools.

  • rag_search -> performs hybrid search (BM25+semantic) over 10000s of documents
  • rag_reflect_context -> it fetches the full text of stored chunks (including structured table data for table-type chunks), generates a partial answer per chunk via parallel LLM calls, and returns those partial answers for agent to compose into a polished response.
  • rag_lookup_pages -> searches result references specific pages agent need more context from, or when the user asks about a specific page (optional for agent)
  • rag_list_documents -> to explore what documents exists in the knowledegbase (optional for agent)

Agent may or may not take the optional steps, as it depends upon the context it has gathered.

Observation:

There is a way to turn off the reasoningEffort via adding the providerOptions in defaultOptions and there are two ways to do this:

  1. Via runtime in .stream() or .generate() method
  2. While initialising the agent with Agent()
providerOptions of Supervisor Agent | providerOptions of Sub-agent | Expected outcome | Actual outcome | Pass/Fail -- | -- | -- | -- | -- { reasoningEffort: 'medium' } set while initialising the agent | { reasoningEffort: 'medium' } | Sub-agent uses reasoning and iterate over tools | Sub-agent uses reasoning and iterate over tools | ✅ { reasoningEffort: 'none' } set while initialising the agent | { reasoningEffort: 'medium' } | Sub-agent uses reasoning and iterate over tools | Sub-agent calls tools only a single time and does not iterate over them | ❌ { reasoningEffort: 'medium' } set via runtime in .stream() | { reasoningEffort: 'medium' } | Sub-agent uses reasoning and iterate over tools | Sub-agent uses reasoning and iterate over tools | ✅ { reasoningEffort: 'none' } set via runtime in .stream() | { reasoningEffort: 'medium' } | Sub-agent uses reasoning and iterate over tools | Sub-agent calls tools only a single time and does not iterate over them | ❌

Passing providerOptions:

Via .stream():

const response = await agentInstance.stream(messages, {
	requestContext: context as RequestContext<AgentContext>,
	memory: {
		thread: {
			id: chat.id,
			metadata: {
				user_id: user.id,
				agent_id: agent.id,
				agent_name: agent.name,
				chat_id: chat.id,
			},
		},
		resource: user.id,
	},
	maxSteps: 10,
	providerOptions: {
		openai: { 
			reasoningEffort: 'none',                    // or 'medium'
			store: false,
			include: ['reasoning.encrypted_content']
		},
	},
});

Via Agent():

export function createRagSubAgent(model: MastraModelConfig): Agent {
    return new Agent({
        id: SUB_AGENT_ID,
        name: 'RAG Document Assistant',
        description:
            'Document research assistant over a knowledge base. Use for questions about uploaded documents: search, reflect, look up pages, or list documents.',
        instructions: RAG_SYSTEM_PROMPT,
        model,
        tools: {
            rag_search: ragSearchTool,
            rag_lookup_pages: ragLookupPagesTool,
            rag_list_documents: ragListDocumentsTool,
            rag_reflect_context: ragReflectContextTool,
        },
        defaultOptions: {
            providerOptions: {
                openai: {
                    reasoningEffort: 'medium',       // always set to 'medium'
                    textVerbosity: 'medium',
                }
            },
        },
    });
}

How would I know that sub-agent isn't reasoning?

Via the answer quality and no. of tools invocations. When setting both supervisor & sub-agent reasoningEffort to medium I got detailed answers and could see sub-agent calling particularly rag_search & rag_reflect_context multiple times to get more relevant answer which is expected. Here the RAG_SYSTEM_PROMPT was same throughout the tests.

End goal:

To reduce the overall latency of the flow and TTFT for better user experience by using reasoning capability of the model only for sub-agents.

Minimal reproduction of the issue:

I’m unable to share a minimal reproducible example due to internal/closed-source dependencies. I have tried to share the code as much as possible and how I have framed it. I understand that makes debugging harder, so I’m happy to provide more additional details, logs, or runs if needed.
@om-scogo hi Om! Can you please update`@mastra/core` to `1.23.0` and confirm you are seeing the same behavior?

title: [BUG] `@hono/zod-openapi` breaks Mastra's type inference

body:
### Describe the Bug

Hey, ran into a weird one and took a while to track down.

I'm using Mastra agents behind an HTTP endpoint and consuming the SSE stream on the frontend with AI SDK's useChat + DefaultChatTransport. Everything was working fine until I added BatchPartsProcessor to outputProcessors — after that, assistant messages stopped rendering in the UI for anything longer than a short response.

After digging into the raw SSE stream I found the issue: when BatchPartsProcessor batches multiple text-delta chunks together, the combined chunk it emits has id: "text-1" hardcoded instead of the real message ID. The AI SDK links text-delta events to the open text part by id, so when it sees a text-start with id: msg_abc123 followed by a text-delta with id: text-1, it just... drops the delta. Message stays empty.

Why it only breaks on longer responses: when the batch has a single item, flushBatch returns the original chunk unchanged (correct ID preserved). When it has multiple items it creates a new synthetic chunk with the hardcoded ID. So anything shorter

### Steps To Reproduce

```
export const agent = new Agent({
  id: 'my-agent',
  model: 'openai/gpt-4o',
  outputProcessors: [new BatchPartsProcessor({ batchSize: 10 })],
})

```
Consume via HTTP SSE + AI SDK useChat. Send a message that gets a response longer than 10 tokens. Watch it not render.

In the raw SSE stream you'll see:

```
data: {"type":"text-start","id":"msg_abc123",...}
data: {"type":"text-delta","id":"text-1","delta":"Here is the..."} ← wrong id
```


### Link to Minimal Reproducible Example

N/A

### Expected Behavior

N/A

### Environment Information

```block
Environment
Mastra

@mastra/core: 1.18.0
@mastra/memory: 1.11.0
@mastra/ai-sdk: 1.3.0
mastra (CLI): 1.3.16
AI SDK (frontend)

ai: ^6.0.141
@ai-sdk/react: (used via useChat; version is whatever resolves from your lockfile)
Runtime / tooling

Node.js: v24.13.0 (from your terminal output)
Package manager: pnpm@10.30.1 (repo root package.json)
OS: macOS (Darwin)
Setup

Architecture: separate services
Agents service (Mastra) streams SSE over HTTP (e.g. /chat/:agentId)
Console frontend consumes SSE via useChat + DefaultChatTransport (AI SDK)
Streaming mode: enabled (SSE / incremental chunks)
```

### Verification

- [ ] I have searched the existing issues to make sure this is not a duplicate
- [x] I have included sufficient information for the team to reproduce and understand the issue

title: Realtime Voice Agent

body:
This issue was created from Discord post 1488113573665177621:

[![Open in Discord](https://img.shields.io/badge/Open_in_Discord-5865F2?style=for-the-badge&logo=discord&logoColor=white)](https://discord.com/channels/1309558646228779139/1488113573665177621)

We noticed that `LibSQLVector.query()` uses brute-force cosine similarity via `vector_distance_cos()` with a full table scan, even though `createIndex()` already creates a `libsql_vector_idx` DiskANN index on the table. The index exists but is never used for queries.

LibSQL supports `vector_top_k(indexName, vector, topK)` which leverages the DiskANN index for approximate nearest neighbor search. We tested this on our dataset (~9K rows, 768-dim embeddings) and saw significant improvement:

- **Brute-force** (`vector_distance_cos`): ~8s for a 4-query tool call
- **DiskANN** (`vector_top_k`): ~0.7s for the same call (~12x faster)
- DB file size trade-off: ~115MB → ~2.8GB

The ~0.7s is mostly the embedding API call — the actual vector search is near-instant.

We worked around this by subclassing `LibSQLVector` and overriding `query()` with:

```sql
SELECT v.id AS rowid,
       vector_distance_cos(t.embedding, vector32(?)) AS distance,
       t.vector_id, t.metadata
FROM vector_top_k('{indexName}_vector_idx', vector32(?), ?) AS v
JOIN "{indexName}" AS t ON t.rowid = v.id
```
One trade-off: vector_top_k() doesn't support inline WHERE clauses, so metadata filtering would need to over-fetch and post-filter. The file size increase (~25x) is also notable.

Would it make sense for LibSQLVector to use vector_top_k() by default when the index exists? Or perhaps as a configurable option (e.g. indexedSearch: true)?
